verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And now, brethren, I commend you to God, and to the word of His grace, which is able to build you up, and to give you an inheritance among all them that are sanctified.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What he does when writing in an Epistle, this he does also when speaking in council: from exhorting, he ends with prayer: for since he had much alarmed them by saying, “Grievous wolves shall enter in among you” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29), therefore, not to overpower them, and make them lose all self-possession, observe the consolation (he gives).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And now,” he says, as always, “I commend you, brethren, to God, and to the word of His grace:” that is, to His grace: it is grace that saveth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He constantly puts them in mind of grace, to make them more earnest as being debtors, and to persuade them to have confidence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which is able to build you up.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, to build, but, “to build up,” showing that they had (already) been built.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he puts them in mind of the hope to come; “to give you an inheritance,” he says, “among all them which are sanctified.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then exhortation again: “I have coveted no man’s silver, or gold, or apparel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He takes away that which is the root of evils, the love of money.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Silver, or gold,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says not, I have not taken, but, not even “coveted.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No great thing this, but what follows after is great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yea, ye yourselves know, that these hands have ministered unto my necessities, and to them that were with me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have showed you all things, how that so laboring, ye ought to support the weak.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe him employed in work and not simply that, but toiling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “These hands have ministered unto my necessities, and to them that were with me:” so as to put them to shame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how worthily of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he says not, Ye ought to show yourselves superior to money, but what? “to support the weak”—not all indiscriminately—“and to hear the word of the Lord which He spake, It is more blessed to give than to receive.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For lest any one should think that it was spoken with reference to them, and that he gave himself for an ensample, as he elsewhere says, “giving an ensample to you” (Phil. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), he added the declaration of Christ, Who said, “It is more blessed to give than to receive.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He prayed over them while exhorting them: he shows it both by action,—“And when he had thus spoken, he kneeled down, and prayed with them all,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36)—he did not simply pray, but with much feeling: (κατανύξεως): great was the consolation—and by his saying, “I commend you to the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they all wept sore, and fell on Paul’s neck and kissed him, sorrowing most of all for the words which he spake, that they should see his face no more.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He had said, that “grievous wolves should enter in;” had said, “I am pure from the blood of all men:” and yet the thing that grieved them most of all was this, “that they should see him no more:” since indeed it was this that made the war grievous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they accompanied them,” it says, “unto the ship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it came to pass, that after we had torn ourselves from them”—so much did they love him, such was their affection towards him—“and had launched, we came with a straight course unto Coos, and the day following unto Rhodes, and from thence unto Patara: and finding a ship sailing over unto Phenicia, we went aboard, and set forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now when we had discovered Cyprus, we left it on the left hand, and sailed into Syria, and landed at Tyre” (Acts xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1–3): he came to Lycia, and having left Cyprus, he sailed down to Tyre—“for there the ship was to unlade her burden.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And finding disciples, we tarried there seven days: who said to Paul through the Spirit, that he should not go up to Jerusalem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They too prophesy of the afflictions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is so ordered that they should be spoken by them also, that none might imagine that Paul said those things without cause, and only by way of boasting.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there again they part from each other with prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when we had accomplished those days, we departed, and went our way; and they all brought us on our way, with wives and children, till we were out of the city: and we kneeled down on the shore, and prayed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we had taken our leave one of another, we took ship; and they returned home again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we had finished our course from Tyre, we came to Ptolemais, and saluted the brethren, and abode with them one day.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the next day we that were of Paul’s company departed, and came unto Cæsarea: and we entered into the house of Philip the evangelist, which was one of the seven; and abode with him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having come to Cæsarea, it says, we abode with Philip, which was one of the seven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the same man had four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is not these that foretell to Paul, though they were prophetesses; it is Agabus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as we tarried there many days, there came down from Judea a certain prophet, named Agabus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he was come unto us, he took Paul’s girdle, and bound his own hands and feet, and said, Thus saith the Holy Ghost, So shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man that owneth this girdle, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who formerly had declared about the famine, the same says, This “man, who owneth this girdle, thus shall they bind.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same that the prophets used to do, representing events to the sight, when they spoke about the captivity—as did Ezekiel—the same did this (Agabus).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” what is the grievous part of the business, “deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we heard these things, both we, and they of that place, besought him not to go up to Jerusalem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many even besought him not to depart, and still he would not comply.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul answered, What mean ye to weep and to break mine heart?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lest, having heard that saying, “I go bound in the Spirit” (ch. xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22), you should imagine it a matter of necessity, or that he fell into it ignorantly, therefore these things are foretold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they wept, and he comforted them, grieving at their tears.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “what mean ye,” he says, “to weep and to break my heart?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing could be more affectionate: because he saw them weeping, he grieved, he that felt no pain at his own trials.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I am ready not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he would not be persuaded, we ceased, saying, The will of the Lord be done.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye do me wrong in doing this: for do I grieve?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then they ceased, when he said, “to break my heart.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I weep, he says, for you, not on account of my own sufferings: as for those (men), I am willing even to die for them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Silver, or gold, or apparel,” etc. (ch. xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 33, 34; 1 Cor. ix; 2 Cor. xi.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, it was not in Corinth only that they did this—they that corrupted the disciples, but in Asia as well.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he nowhere casts this up as a reproach to the Ephesians, when writing to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because he did not fall upon any subject that obliged him to speak of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But to the Corinthians he says, “My boasting has not been stopped in the regions of Achaia.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, Ye did not give to me; but, “Silver, or gold, or apparel, I coveted not,” that it might not seem to be their doing, that they had not given.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, From no man have I coveted the necessaries of life, that again it might not look like accusing them: but he covertly hints as much, seeing that he provided subsistence for others as well as himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he worked with earnestness, “night and day” discoursing (to others), “with tears warning each one of them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Here) again he puts them in fear: “I have showed you all things,” he says: ye cannot take refuge in the plea of ignorance: “have shown you” by works “how that so laboring ye ought to work.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, that to receive is bad, but that not to receive is better.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “remember,” he says, “the words of the Lord which he spake: It is more blessed to give than to receive.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And where said He this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps the Apostles delivered it by unwritten tradition; or else it is plain from (recorded sayings, from) which one could infer it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in fact here he has shown both boldness in meeting dangers, sympathy with those over whom he ruled, teaching with (unshrinking) boldness, humility, (voluntary) poverty: but, what we have here is even more than that poverty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if He says there (in the Gospel), “If thou wilt be perfect, sell what thou hast and give to the poor” (Matt. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21), when, besides receiving nothing himself, he provides sustenance for others also, what could equal this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is one degree to fling away one’s possessions; a second, to be sufficient for the supply of one’s own necessities: a third, to provide for others also; a fourth, for one (to do all this) who preaches and has a right to receive.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that here is a man far better than those who merely forego possessions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thus it is right to support the weak:” this is (indeed) sympathy with the weak; for to give from the labors of others, is easy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they fell on his neck,” it says, “and wept.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows their affection also by saying, “Upon his neck,” as taking a last and yet a last embrace, such was the love they conceived from his discourse, such the spell of love that bound them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if we groan when simply parting from each other, although we know that we shall receive one another back again, what a tearing away of themselves it must have been to them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Methinks Paul also wept.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having torn ourselves away,” he says: he shows the violence of it by saying, “having torn ourselves away from them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with reason: otherwise they could never have got to sea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “We came with a straight course unto Coos?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Instead of saying, “we did not go round nor make stay in other places.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then “unto Rhodes.” (ch. xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he hastes on.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And finding a ship sailing over unto Phenicia. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Possibly that ship (in which they had come) was making a stay there: wherefore they shifted to another, and not having found one going to Cæsarea, but (finding this) for Phenice, they embarked in it (and pursued their voyage), having left Cyprus also and Syria: but the expression, “having left it on the left hand,” is not said simply (in that meaning), but that they made speed not to get to Syria either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We landed at Tyre.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then they tarry with the brethren seven days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now that they were come near to Jerusalem, they no longer run. (b) “Who said to Paul through the Spirit, that he should not go up to Jerusalem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, when the Spirit does not forbid, he complies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They said, “Adventure not thyself into the theatre, and he did not adventure” (ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31): often they bore him off (from dangers), and he complied: again he escaped by a window: and now, though numberless persons, so to say, beseech him, both those at Tyre and those at Cæsarea, weeping also and predicting numberless dangers, he refuses to comply.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it is not (merely), they predicted the dangers, but “said by the Spirit.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then the Spirit bade, why did he gainsay?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “By the Spirit,” that is, they knowing “by the Spirit” (what would be the consequences, said to him): for of course it does not mean that the exhortation they made was by the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they did not simply foretell to him the dangers (through the Spirit), but (added of themselves) that it behooved him not to go up—sparing him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But “after we had accomplished the days,” i.e. had fulfilled the appointed days, “we separated, and went on our way: they all bringing us on our way with wives and children.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5.)—See how great was the entreaty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again they part with prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also in Ptolemais they stay one day, but in Cæsarea many. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6–8.) (a) Now that they are near to Jerusalem, they no longer hurry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For observe, I pray you, all the days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “After the day of unleavened bread” they came “to Troas in five days” (ch. xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6); then they there spent “seven;” in all, twelve: then to “Thasos,” to “Mytilene,” to “Trogylium” and “over against Chios,” and to “Samos” and “Miletus” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13–17); eighteen in all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then to “Cos,” to “Rhodes,” to “Patara,” twenty-one: then say five to “Tyre;” twenty-six: there “seven;” thirty-three; “Ptolemais,” thirty-four; then to “Cæsarea, many days” (ch. xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1–10); and then, thereafter, the prophet puts them up thence. (c) When Paul has heard that he has to suffer numberless perils, then he is in haste, not flinging himself upon the dangers but accounting it to be the command of the Spirit. (e) And Agabus does not say, “They shall bind” Paul, that he may not seem to speak upon agreement (with Paul), but “the man that owneth this girdle” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11)—so then he had a girdle also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when they could not persuade him—this was why they wept—then they “held their peace.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the resignation? do you mark the affection?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They held their peace,” it says, “saying, The will of the Lord be done.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12–14.) (g) The Lord, say they, Himself will do that which is pleasing in his sight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they perceived that it was the will of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else Paul would not be so bent (upon going)—he that on all (other occasions delivers himself out of dangers. (d) “And after these, days,” it says, “having taken up our baggage”—i.e. having received the (supplies) necessary for the journey—“we went up to Jerusalem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there went with us also certain of the disciples from Cæsarea, bringing us to one with whom we should lodge, one Mnason, an ancient disciple of Cyprus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when we were come to Jerusalem, the brethren received us gladly.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17.) (f) “Bringing us,” it says, “(to him) with whom we should lodge”—not to the church: for on the former occasion (ch. xv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4), when they went up concerning the decrees, they lodged with the Church, but now with a certain “ancient disciple.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (The expression) shows that the preaching had been going on a long time: whence it seems to me that this writer in the Acts epitomizes the events of many years, relating (only) the matters of chief importance. (h) So unwilling were they to burthen the Church, when there was another to lodge them; and so little did they stand upon their dignity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The brethren,” it says, “received us gladly.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Affairs among the Jews were now full of peace: there was not much warfare (among them).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Bringing us,” it says, “to one with whom we should lodge.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul was the guest he entertained.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perchance some one of you says: Aye, if it were given me to entertain Paul as a guest, I readily and with much eagerness would do this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! it is in thy power to entertain Paul’s Master for thy guest, and thou wilt not: for “he that receiveth one of these least,” he saith, “receiveth Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By how much the brother may be least, so much the more does Christ come to thee through him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that receives the great, often does it from vainglory also; but he that receives the small, does it purely for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is in thy power to entertain even the Father of Christ as thy guest, and thou will not: for, “I was a stranger,” He says, “and ye took me in” (Matt. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 35): and again, “Unto one of the least of these the brethren that believe on Me, ye have done it unto Me.” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though it be not Paul, yet if it be a believer and a brother, although the least, Christ cometh to thee through him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Open thine house, take Him in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He that receiveth a prophet,” He saith, “shall receive a prophet’s reward.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore too he that receives Christ, shall receive the reward of him who has Christ for his guest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not thou disbelieve His words, but be believing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Himself hath said, Through them I come to thee: and that thou mayest not disbelieve, He lays down both punishments for those who do not receive, and honors for those who do receive; since He would not have done this, unless both the person honored and the person insulted were Himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou receivedst Me,” He saith, “into thy lodging, I will receive thee into the Kingdom of My Father; thou tookest away My hunger, I take away thy sins; thou sawest Me bound, I see thee loosed; thou sawest Me a stranger, I make thee a citizen of heaven; thou gavest Me bread, I give thee an entire Kingdom, that thou mayest inherit and possess it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saith not, “Receive,” but, “Inherit,” the word which is spoken of those who have possession by right of ownership; as when we say, “This have I inherited.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou didst it to Me in secret, I will proclaim it openly: and of thine acts indeed I say, that they were of free gift, but Mine are of debt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For since thou,” He saith, “didst begin, I follow and come after: I am not ashamed to confess the benefits conferred on Me, nor from what things thou didst free Me, hunger and nakedness and wandering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou sawest Me bound, thou shalt not behold the fire of hell; thou sawest Me sick, thou shalt not behold the torments nor the punishments.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O hands, truly blessed, which minister in such services as these, which are accounted worthy to serve Christ!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Feet which go into prisons for Christ’s sake, with ease defy the fire: no trial of bonds have they, (the hands) which saw Him bound!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou clothedst Him with a garment, and thou puttest on a garment of salvation: thou wast in prison with Him, and with Him thou findest thyself in the Kingdom, not ashamed, knowing that thou visitedst Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Patriarch knew not that he was entertaining Angels, and he did entertain them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Gen. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us take shame to ourselves, I beseech you: he was sitting in mid-day, being in a foreign land, where he had none inheritance, “not so much as to set his foot on” (ch. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5): he was a stranger, and the stranger entertained strangers: for he was a citizen of heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, not even while he was on earth was he a stranger (to Him).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are rather strangers than that stranger, if we receive not strangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He had no home, and his tent was his place of reception.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And mark his liberality—he killed a calf, and kneaded fine meal: mark his ready mind—by himself and his wife: mark the unassuming manner—he worships and beseeches them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For all these qualities ought to be in that man who entertains strangers—readiness, cheerfulness, liberality.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the soul of the stranger is abashed, and feels ashamed; and unless (his host) show excessive joy, he is as (if) slighted, and goes away, and it becomes worse than not to have received him, his being received in this way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore he worships them, therefore he welcomes them with speech, therefore with a seat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who would have hesitated, knowing that this work was done unto Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But we are not in a foreign land.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we will, we shall be able to imitate him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many of the brethren are strangers?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is a common apartment, the Church, which we call the “Xenon.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be inquisitive (περιεργάζεσθε), sit before the doors, receive those who come yourselves; though you may not wish to take them into your houses, at any rate in some other way (receive them), by supplying them with necessaries.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Why, has not the Church means” you will say?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: She has: but what is that to you? that they should be fed from the common funds of the Church, can that benefit you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If another man prays, does it follow that you are not bound to pray?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore do you not say, “Do not the priests pray? then why should I pray?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But I,” you will say, “give to him who cannot be received there.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Give, though it be to that one: for what we are anxious for is this, that you should give at any rate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear what Paul says: “That it may relieve them that are widows indeed, and that the Church be not burdened.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be it how you will, only do it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I put it, not, “that the Church be not burdened,” but, “that thou be not burdened;” for at this rate thou wilt do nothing, leaving all to the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why there is a common room set apart by the Church, that you may not say these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The Church,” say you, “has lands, has money, and revenues.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And has she not charges?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I ask; and has she not a daily expenditure?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “No doubt,” you will say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then do you not lend aid to her moderate means?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am ashamed indeed to say these things: however, I compel no man, if any one imagines what I am saying to be for gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make for yourself a guest-chamber in your own house: set up a bed there, set up a table there and a candlestick. (comp.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For is it not absurd, that whereas, if soldiers should come, you have rooms set apart for them, and show much care for them, and furnish them with everything, because they keep off from you the visible war of this world, yet strangers have no place where they might abide?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Gain a victory over the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you put us to shame?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This do: surpass us in liberality: have a room, to which Christ may come; say, “This is Christ’s cell; this building is set apart for Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be it but an underground chamber, and mean, He disdains it not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Naked and a stranger,” Christ goes about, it is but a shelter He wants: afford it, though but this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be not uncompassionate, nor inhuman; be not so earnest in worldly matters, so cold in spiritual.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let also the most faithful of thy servants be the one entrusted with this office, and let him bring in the maimed, the beggars, and the homeless.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things I say to shame you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ye ought indeed to receive them in the upper part of your house; but if ye will not do this, then though it be below, though but where thy mules are housed, and thy servants, there receive Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perchance ye shudder at hearing this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then, when ye do not even this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, I exhort, behold, I bid you; let this be a matter to be taken up in earnest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But ye do not wish it thus, perhaps?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do it some other way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are many poor men and poor women: set apart some one (of these) constantly to remain there: let the poor man be (thine inmate) though but as a guard to thy house: let him be to thee wall and fence, shield and spear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where alms are, the devil dares not approach, nor any other evil thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not overlook so great a gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now a place is set apart for a chariot, and for litters (βαστερνίοις) another; but for Christ Who is wandering, not even one!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Abraham received the strangers in the place where he abode himself; his wife stood in the place of a servant, the guests in the place of masters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He knew not that he was receiving Christ; knew not that he was receiving Angels; so that had he known it, he would have lavished his whole substance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we, who know that we receive Christ, show not even so much zeal as he did who thought that he was receiving men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But they are impostors,” you will say, “many of them, and unthankful.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And for this the greater thy reward, when thou receivest for the sake of Christ’s name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if thou knowest indeed that they are impostors, receive them not into thy house: but if thou dost not know this, why dost thou accuse them lightly?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore I tell them to go to the receiving house.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what kind of excuse is there for us, when we do not even receive those whom we know, but shut our doors against all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let our house be Christ’s general receptacle: let us demand of them as a reward, not money, but that they make our house the receptacle for Christ; let us run about everywhere, let us drag them in, let us seize our booty: greater are the benefits we receive than what we confer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not bid thee kill a calf: give thou bread to the hungry, raiment to the naked, shelter to the stranger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that thou mayest not make this thy pretext, there is a common apartment, that of the Church; throw thy money into that, and then thou hast received them: since (Abraham) there had the reward of those things also which were done by his servants.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He gave the calf to a young man, and he hasted to dress it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Gen. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So well trained were his servants also!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They ran, and murmured not as ours do: for he had made them pious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He drew them out to war, and they murmured not: so well disciplined were they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he had equal care for all as for himself: he all but said as Job did, “We were alike formed in the same womb.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Job xxxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore let us also take thought for their salvation, and let us make it our duty to care for our servants, that they may be good; and let our servants also be instructed in the things pertaining to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then will virtue not be difficult to us, if we train them orderly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as in war, when the soldiers are well-disciplined, the general carries on war easily, but the contrary happens, when this is not so; and when the sailors too are of one mind, the pilot easily handles the rudder-strings; so here likewise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say now, if thy servants have been so schooled, thou wilt not be easily exasperated, thou wilt not have to find fault, wilt not be made angry, wilt not need to abuse them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It may be, thou wilt even stand in awe of thy servants, if they are worthy of admiration, and they will be helpers with thee, and will give thee good counsel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But from all these shall all things proceed that are pleasing to God, and thus shall the whole house be filled with blessing, and we, performing things pleasing to God, shall enjoy abundant succor from above, unto which may we all attain, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost, together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
